desc: The mutable default headers dictionary is modified in place, causing request headers to leak across calls.
fetch_resource mutates the caller-provided headers dictionary by assigning User-Agent.
fetch_resource always overwrites User-Agent, so batch_fetch's mobile, bot, and desktop modes have no effect.
The cache is keyed only by URL, so requests with different headers, authentication, or redirect policies can receive an incompatible cached response.
The process-wide response cache has no size limit or eviction policy and can grow indefinitely.
Caching and returning the same mutable Response object lets one caller's consumption, closure, or mutation affect later callers.
All HTTP requests omit a timeout and can block indefinitely on an unresponsive server.
HTTP error responses are treated as successful data because status codes are not validated before caching, hashing, or writing content.
download_file never closes its streamed Response, potentially leaking connections and exhausting the connection pool.
download_file accumulates the entire response in memory despite enabling streaming, allowing large downloads to exhaust memory.
Repeated content += chunk copies the growing byte string on every iteration, producing quadratic time and allocation behavior.
The preview limit is checked before appending and uses > rather than >=, so the written preview can substantially exceed 3000 bytes.
fetch_and_verify hashes decoded and re-encoded text rather than the original response bytes, so its checksum may not represent the fetched resource.
fetch_and_verify only computes a checksum and never compares it with an expected value, so it does not verify integrity.
MD5 is collision-prone and unsuitable if the reported checksum is used for security-sensitive integrity verification.
Printing r.request.headers can disclose sensitive headers from a cached request, such as Authorization or cookies.
Defining hash shadows Python's built-in hash function, making the module API misleading and error-prone.
